---
title: "Eriksen Flanker Experiment"
author: "Rubee Kung, Nicole Hua, Jacob Grima, Eda Kubra Ozkaya"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: moon   
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    incremental: true
---

## Introduction

- First bullet point
- Second bullet point

## History & Background
- First bullet point
- Second bullet point

## Methods
- Design
    - Within-subject design
- Conditions:
    - Congruent vs. Incongruent
    - Left vs. Right
- Participants
    - N = 16
    - Recruited from classmates
- Measures
    - Reaction time (ms)
    - Accuracy (%)

## Procedure & Task
- Procedure
    - 4 practice trials (with feedback)
    - 20 experimental trials (no feedback)
    - Trials randomly generated
- Stimuli & Response
    - Arrow strings (e.g., <<<<<, >><>>)
    - Respond to the center arrow only
- Trial Structure
    - Fixation (500 ms)
    - Stimulus (max 2000 ms)
    - Inter-trial interval (700 ms)
- Implementation
    - Implemented in Python using pygame
    - Automatically recorded RT and accuracy

## Results 
- For our Flanker task, we ran a repeated measures ANOVA
    - Independent variable: congruency 
    - Dependent variable: response time 
- Removed inaccurate trials
- Our ANOVA gave us F(1,15) = 56.70, p<.001

## ANOVA Figure 
![](ANOVAfigure.png)

## Graph Figure 
![](slidesfigure.png)

## Discussion
- The results show that there are differences in response time depending on the congruency of the trials 
- Incongruent trials has longer reaction times than congruent trials 
- We reject the null hypothesis 

## Conclusion
- Our findings align with typical Erikson Flanker Task studies
- Arrow congruency significantly affects response time, supporting the flanker interference effect
- Future research could explore individual differences in cognitive control between different demographics
